subject: ap-csp
theme: Algorithms and programming involve using sequencing, selection, and iteration with CB pseudocode to build efficient, abstract solutions — including understanding what problems computers cannot solve.
core_concepts: CB pseudocode uses ← for assignment (not =), DISPLAY(), IF/ELSE, REPEAT n TIMES, REPEAT UNTIL, FOR EACH, and PROCEDURE with RETURN.
Lists are 1-indexed; key operations: APPEND(list, val), INSERT(list, i, val), REMOVE(list, i), LENGTH(list).
Linear search checks every element sequentially (works on any list); binary search halves the search space each step (requires sorted list, max log₂(n) steps).
Procedural abstraction hides implementation details behind a named procedure — callers only need to know what it does, not how.
Algorithms that run in polynomial time (n, n², n³) are considered to run in reasonable time (tractable); exponential time algorithms are often impractical.
The halting problem is undecidable — no algorithm can determine for all programs whether they will eventually halt.
Simulations model real-world processes with simplified rules; RANDOM(a, b) returns a random integer between a and b inclusive.
exam_tip: Unit 3 is 30–35% of the exam — trace pseudocode carefully (remember ← is assignment, not comparison), know list indexing starts at 1, and be able to identify when binary vs. linear search is more efficient.
